ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 covers shrimp whole or parts; de-shelled prawn preparation is not a distinct FoodOn class. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GR covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific de-shelled sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 refers to prawn as a substance; de-shelled preparation is not separately coded. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: raw or lightly cooked depending on restaurant preparation
may be steamed, stir-fried, or grilled

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of de-shelled prawns in Singapore is approximately 200 g of peeled prawn meat, often served as part of a shared seafood dish.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, raw (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 175180, SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw shrimp (FDC ID 175180) used as the primary nutrient reference for de-shelled prawns. De-shelled prawns are nutritionally equivalent to raw peeled shrimp per 100 g of edible portion. Values represent the raw edible portion; actual cooked values at the restaurant may vary slightly depending on preparation method (steaming, stir-frying, grilling). Sodium may be higher if seasoning is applied.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Prawns and shrimp are very low-carbohydrate foods (0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g). No direct GI measurement exists for de-shelled prawns in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed tables, as pure protein-fat foods are not typically tested. By analogy with other very low-carbohydrate seafoods and protein foods, GI is estimated at 2 (effectively negligible). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (2 × 0.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.008, rounded to 0.0. gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Naturally high sodium content in raw prawns (~566 mg/100 g); restaurant preparation with added seasoning, sauces, or salt can substantially increase sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g restaurant serving of de-shelled prawns may contribute approximately 1,130 mg sodium from the prawn alone, and should request minimal added salt or sauces to keep total sodium within the recommended 2,000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.2 g/100 g) and very low glycemic index (~2); high protein content supports satiety without raising postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: De-shelled prawns are an excellent protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their negligible carbohydrate content and very low glycemic index, making them unlikely to cause postprandial glucose excursions when consumed without high-carbohydrate sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content (~161 mg/100 g) alongside beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and low saturated fat; net cardiovascular effect depends on overall dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While de-shelled prawns contain moderate dietary cholesterol, their low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids make them acceptable in a heart-healthy diet when consumed in moderate portions and prepared without butter or high-fat sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: De-Shelled Prawns (虾球, Xia Qiu) are whole prawns that have been fully peeled and de-veined, leaving only the tender prawn meat. In Singapore's Chinese seafood restaurant context, this dish is typically served as a premium seafood item where the labour-intensive shelling has been done for the diner, allowing easy consumption. The prawns may be prepared by steaming, stir-frying with garlic and ginger, grilling, or served in a light sauce. The texture is firm yet succulent, with a naturally sweet, briny flavour characteristic of fresh marine prawns. Nutritionally, de-shelled prawns are an excellent high-protein, low-fat, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 85 kcal and 20 g protein per 100 g of edible portion. They are rich in selenium, phosphorus, potassium, vitamin B12, and vitamin A. The cholesterol content (~161 mg/100 g) is notable but the saturated fat is very low (0.2 g/100 g), making them suitable for most dietary patterns in moderate portions. The glycemic index is negligible, making them ideal for blood glucose management. Sodium content is naturally elevated in prawns (~566 mg/100 g) and may increase further with restaurant seasoning. This dish is commonly ordered as part of a shared Chinese seafood meal in Singapore, often alongside white bee hoon, steamed fish, or other shellfish dishes. It is halal-certified in most Singapore seafood restaurants and is gluten-free and dairy-free by nature.
common_user_queries: How many calories in de-shelled prawns?
Is 虾球 high in cholesterol?
Are shelled prawns good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw shrimp data (FDC ID 175180), which is a well-validated primary source for peeled shrimp. The match is adapted rather than exact because the field survey listing refers to a restaurant-prepared dish where seasoning and cooking method may alter final nutrient values, particularly sodium. GI is estimated by analogy as no direct GI measurement exists for de-shelled prawns in approved GI databases. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for raw shrimp (FDC ID 175180). 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy; confirmed zero per source. 3. Sugar is reported as 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy; confirmed zero per source. 4. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy; confirmed zero per source — prawns contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). 5. Carbohydrate_g is 0.2 g (not zero); no zero-macro justification required for carbohydrate. 6. GI is estimated at 2 (effectively negligible) as de-shelled prawns contain only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and no direct GI measurement exists in approved sources; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. 7. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (2 × 0.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.008, rounded to 0.0. 8. Sodium value of 566 mg/100 g reflects the natural sodium content of raw shrimp per USDA; restaurant-prepared versions with added seasoning may be significantly higher. 9. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175180. 10. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 11. The cuisine_tag 'Crab' in the input appears to be a platform tagging artefact; this record pertains to prawns, not crab.
